name: Django
what_it_does: Full-featured Python web framework with batteries included: ORM, admin interface, authentication, templating, forms, and security middleware. Django REST Framework (DRF) adds API capabilities with serializers, viewsets, routers, and browsable API. Agents typically use Django + DRF to build the API backends they expose, or interact with Django-built APIs.
use_cases: Building full-featured API backends with built-in ORM, auth, and admin that agents consume
Data-heavy applications where Django's ORM and migrations simplify database management
Applications requiring a content management admin interface alongside an API
Multi-tenant SaaS backends where Django's auth and permission system handles access control
Rapid development of CRUD-heavy APIs using DRF viewsets and routers
not_for: Pure async high-performance APIs where FastAPI or Starlette outperform Django's sync-first design
Microservices that need minimal footprint — Django is heavyweight for single-purpose services
Agents that need to consume an external API — Django is for building backends, not calling third-party APIs
best_when: You are building a data-driven Python API with complex data models, authentication, and admin needs, and want a battle-tested framework with years of security patches. DRF is the go-to for Django-based REST APIs.
avoid_when: You need high-throughput async performance (use FastAPI + async ORM), or are building a microservice that doesn't need Django's full stack (ORM, admin, auth, etc.). The overhead is unnecessary for simple APIs.

auth.notes: Django has built-in session and token authentication. DRF adds TokenAuthentication and SessionAuthentication out of the box. djangorestframework-simplejwt adds JWT. django-oauth-toolkit adds OAuth2 with full scopes support. Permission classes in DRF provide object-level and model-level authorization. Auth is comprehensive and production-ready.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Completely free and open source (BSD-3-Clause). No usage limits.
pricing.paid_tiers: None for the framework. Deploy on any Python hosting.
pricing.notes: Django is maintained by the Django Software Foundation, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit. BSD license with no commercial restrictions. Django REST Framework has a paid sponsorship model but the software is MIT licensed and free to use.

performance.rate_limits: No built-in rate limiting in Django. DRF has throttle classes (AnonRateThrottle, UserRateThrottle) configurable in settings. Django 4.1+ has limited async view support; full async ORM came in Django 4.1 but sync is still the default pattern.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: DRF returns structured validation errors as JSON objects with field names as keys and error arrays as values — very agent-parseable. Non-field errors use non_field_errors key. HTTP 400 responses include detailed validation context. Django's own error pages are HTML by default — DRF exception handler overrides this to JSON for API views. Consistency depends on developer use of DRF exception handler.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No framework-level idempotency support. Django does not provide idempotency keys. Developers must implement at the view level. DRF's UpdateModelMixin for PUT is idempotent by REST semantics but no header-based idempotency keys.
known_agent_gotchas: Django generates HTML error pages by default — DRF must be configured as the exception handler to return JSON errors for all API endpoints
CSRF protection is enabled by default — API clients must use SessionAuthentication + CSRF token or switch to TokenAuthentication to avoid 403 errors
DRF browsable API adds HTML Accept header handling — agents sending Accept: application/json get clean JSON responses
Django ORM is synchronous — blocking database calls in async contexts require sync_to_async wrappers (added complexity)
DRF ViewSets auto-generate multiple URLs — agents must understand router-generated URL patterns to construct correct endpoint paths
Django migrations must be applied before first use — agents deploying Django apps must run manage.py migrate in deployment
SECRET_KEY must be kept secret — Django generates cryptographic tokens from it; leaking it is a critical security issue

security_score_components.security_notes: Django has one of the strongest security postures of any web framework: CSRF protection, XSS protection, SQL injection prevention via ORM, clickjacking protection, and a dedicated security team issuing CVEs. Security middleware is enabled by default. SECRET_KEY management is the primary developer responsibility. Django's security changelog is exemplary. HTTPS redirect available via SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT setting.
